file_name: Udriștoiu et al. - 2020 - Skin diseases classification using deep leaning methods.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a custom four-layer Convolutional Neural Network (4-CNN) architecture implemented in Python using Keras and TensorFlow to classify dermatoscopic images of skin lesions into 7 classes (akiec, bcc, bkl, df, mel, nv, vasc) using the HAM10000 dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper is explicitly labeled as an 'Original Paper' and describes a study where the authors proposed and trained a custom four-layer Convolutional Neural Network (4-CNN) architecture. It details the methodology (data augmentation, model architecture, hyperparameters), the dataset used (HAM10000), and reports original experimental results including accuracy (93.6%) and AUC (0.97) metrics, which characterizes it as an original experimental study rather than a review.

title: Skin Diseases Classification Using Deep Leaning Methods
brief_summary: This study developed a custom four-layer Convolutional Neural Network (4-CNN) to classify dermatoscopic images of skin lesions into seven diagnostic categories using the HAM10000 dataset. The model was trained on 23,018 augmented images and tested on 1,249 images to address the challenge of fine-grained variations in skin diseases. Evaluation metrics included accuracy, precision, sensitivity, specificity, and the area under the curve (AUC). The proposed 4-CNN achieved an average test accuracy of 93.6%, an average sensitivity of 95.9%, an average specificity of 98.3%, and an AUC of 0.97. The results indicate that the deep learning algorithm performs well and could assist dermatologists in the diagnostic process, despite limitations regarding dataset imbalance and lack of image standardization.

all_task_types.target: Seven diagnostic categories of skin lesions (akiec, bcc, bkl, df, mel, nv, vasc)
all_task_types.details: The paper describes a multi-class classification problem where a custom four-layer Convolutional Neural Network (4-CNN) classifies dermatoscopic images into 7 discrete diagnostic classes using the HAM10000 dataset. The model predicts discrete labels, and performance is evaluated using accuracy, precision, sensitivity, specificity, and AUC.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000
datasets_used.description: A public dataset of multi-source dermatoscopic images of common pigmented skin lesions, collected from different populations and acquired/stored in different ways. The original dataset consists of 10,015 images. The study used an augmented version of this dataset to address class imbalance.
classes_used: akiec (actinic keratoses and intraepithelial carcinoma/Bowen disease)

use.training: Used for training the custom four-layer Convolutional Neural Network (4-CNN) model after data augmentation.
use.testing: Used for testing the trained 4-CNN model to evaluate performance metrics (accuracy, precision, sensitivity, specificity, AUC).

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the overall performance of all diagnosis classes by computing average values of evaluation metrics (precision, sensitivity, specificity, accuracy) across classes.
To summarize the model's behavior by calculating true positive rate (sensitivity) and false positive rate under different thresholds, and to compute the Area Under the Curve (AUC).
To represent the balance between the true positive rate and the positive predictive value for the predictive model using different probability thresholds.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics such as accuracy, precision, sensitivity, specificity, and AUC.

HAM10000.notes: The model achieved an average test accuracy of 93.6%, average sensitivity of 95.9%, average specificity of 98.3%, and an AUC of 0.97. The ROC curves showed a true positive rate between 0.9 and 1 with a false positive rate close to zero. The Precision-Recall curves indicated high recall and high precision.

training_details.hardware: Intel(R) Xeon(R) CPU E5-1620 v3 @ 3.50GHz, 32 GB RAM, NVIDIA Quadro K4200 GPU

classification_or_output_head.details: Final layer (FC4) outputs 7 classes corresponding to skin lesion types (akiec, bcc, bkl, df, mel, nv, vasc)

preprocessing_techniques: Image resizing: Original images (600x450 pixels) were resized to 64x64 pixels to reduce computational difficulty.
Data augmentation: Applied to address class imbalance, including random rotation, width shift, height shift, shear, and zoom transformations.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied via rotation_range to randomly rotate images.
Applied via width_shift to randomly translate images horizontally.
Applied via height_shift to randomly translate images vertically.
Applied via shear_range to randomly apply shearing transformations.
Applied via zoom_range to randomly zoom inside images.

conclusions: The proposed custom four-layer Convolutional Neural Network (4-CNN) achieved an average test accuracy of 93.6%, average sensitivity of 95.9%, average specificity of 98.3%, and an AUC of 0.97 on the HAM10000 dataset.
The deep learning algorithm demonstrated superior performance compared to traditional machine learning methods by eliminating the need for initial visual feature description or segmentation.

The 4-CNN model showed high precision and recall, with a false positive rate close to zero and a true positive rate between 0.9 and 1, indicating effective classification of the seven skin lesion classes.

The study acknowledges limitations including the risk of overfitting due to the known testing dataset, class imbalance with fewer images for rare tumors, and lack of standardization in dermatoscopic images (varying camera angles, orientations, backgrounds, and lighting).
The authors conclude that the developed deep learning algorithm has the potential to assist dermatologists in the diagnostic process in clinical practice.
important_remarks: The study acknowledges a risk of overfitting because the testing dataset was known to the authors during development.
The dataset is imbalanced, with significantly fewer images for rare tumors (e.g., DF and VASC) compared to common ones (e.g., NV).
Dermatoscopic images lack standardization, varying in camera angles, orientations, skin backgrounds, and lighting conditions.
The model was trained on an augmented dataset (24,267 images) derived from the original HAM10000 dataset (10,015 images) to address class imbalance.
The paper does not report formal statistical hypothesis testing, p-values, or confidence intervals for the performance metrics.

The study compares its custom 4-CNN against traditional machine learning methods but does not provide direct comparative metrics against other deep learning models like ResNet or Inception within its own experimental results.
